chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number:	CSCE 470
Course Title:	Information Storage and Retrieval
Section:	500
Time:	M/W/F, 10:20 am - 11:10 am
Location:	EABB 106
Credit Hours:	3

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Representation of, storage of and access to very large multimedia document collections; fundamental data structures and algorithms of current information storage and retrieval systems and relates various techniques to design and evaluation of complete retrieval systems.

This course will introduce the key concepts, models, algorithms and techniques at the core of modern text-based search systems. The topics covered in this course include efficient text indexing, Boolean, vector space and probabilistic retrieval models, retrieval evaluation, relevance feedback, document classification, learning to rank, document clustering and link analysis. The students will have an opportunity to implement key retrieval models on top of an open-source search engine system.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331 or approval of instructor.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
The main objective of this course is to equip students with solid theoretical knowledge and enough practical experience to develop and diagnose their own search systems in the future. Upon successful completion of the course, a student will be able to:
• Define and explain the key concepts and models relevant to information storage and retrieval.
• Implement important algorithms, recognize and fix common problems in practice.
• Design and develop his/her own text search systems addressing customized information needs.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
The recommended textbook: Introduction to Information Retrieval, Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan, and Hinrich Schutze, Cambridge University Press. 2008.

Instructor will provide copies of lecture slides and links to resources on the internet.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
The course grading policy is as follows: two programming assignments (30%), four written assignments (20%), mid-term exam (20%), class participation (5%) and class project (25%).

The grading scale is: A: 90-100, B: 80-89, C: 70-79, D: 60-69, F: 0-59. Scores are rounded up.

chunks.title: Graded Class Participation
chunks.content: Class participation will be measured through attendance in class, submission of assignments, participation in online discussions and completion of online course evaluation.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Submitting a deliverable after the established deadline is seen as a late submission. For the programming and written homework assignments, we accept late submission subject to the following late work policy. You have a total of 5 late days that you can use during the semester. However, a single assignment can be submitted up to 2 days late only. For the purposes of the class, a late day is an indivisible 24-hour unit. Once you exhaust your 5 late days, we will not accept any late submissions.

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Course Topics, Calendar of Activities, Major Assignment Dates (Tentative)

| Week | Topic |
|---|---|
| 1 | Overview and Vector space model |
| 2 | BM25 |
| 3 | Retrieval Evaluation |
| 4 | Relevance Feedback, #1 programming assignment due |
| 5 | Learning to Rank & language models, #1 written assignment due |
| 6 | Document Classification |
| 7 | Indexing & The Boolean Retrieval model |
| 8 | Flat clustering, #2 Programming assignment due |
| 9 | Hierarchical clustering, #2 written assignment due |
| 10 | Link analysis |
| 11 | Large Language Models, #3 written assignment due |
| 12 | Retrieval Augmented Generation, #4 written assignment due |
| 13 | Other Trending topics |
| 14 | Other Trending topics |
| 15 | Project Presentations |

chunks.title: Artificial Intelligence Statements
chunks.content: This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students themselves. Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any substantive portion of an assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write assignments and using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT.
